speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 18, 1996. I am Tina-- Tina [? Tito, ?] the interviewer. And I will be interviewing Waclaw Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Mr. Kolodziejek--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell your full name for us, your first and second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. W-A-C-A-- A-- A-W. Waclaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-L-O-D-Z-I-E-J-E-K, Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you please say the whole name for us now correctly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolodziejek? Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, Wola Korycka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell the-- the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-- W-O-L-A K-O-R-I-K-A, Korycka.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 28, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family. What is your father's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wincenty Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father build a castle, a cathedral of Georges. And also a baron's palaces.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: His profession was architect, special architect.

speaker: interviewer
text: What university did he go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, I don't know. I believe in [INAUDIBLE], under Russian czar-- czar-- [? what ?] czar. He was very sickly, disciplinary--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- how did you deal with his discipline?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky. We was happy when he went away from home. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you do when he went away from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly playing football, and what kind of game, and ride horses. And beautiful, beautiful life. And then we went to the forest, pick up mushrooms, blueberries, raspberries during the daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you do all these things with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, [INAUDIBLE], Mieczyslaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Younger one, and also with a couple girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to that, let-- tell me about you other brothers and sisters. Could you name them for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Janina and Teresa, and my brother Mieczyslaw and [? Eugene. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are closest with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mieczyslaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play with him all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was different-- different person. I was different from my entire family. I was very full of energy. I do so many crazy things. I went on a tree, apple tree or pear. I fall down, break the branches. I was like a monkey, all-- all-- all around. My mom had so much problem with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever break any parts of your body?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were very energetic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you love to do as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gymnastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do formal gymnastic? Did you have a teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially in the-- when we were in the school, and we do the [? one-hour ?] gymnastic. I was the best one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of gymnastics did you enjoy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Running, pull-- pulling the rope, jumping; things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Swimming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Swimming, not so good. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any other outside sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hiking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiking, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever ski?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not me. I have not-- not the chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Getting back to your father, was he born in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in Wola Korycka, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had a lot of family in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I forgot to tell you, my father been remarried. There was seven of us, seven-- seven from my-- the first mom, which I don't know, because she died. And there was seven from my-- my mom, 14 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- [LAUGHS] No, there was a-- my family was all over in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But these children, did they grow up with you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, mostly I grow up with my mom lately. When-- and when Marshal Pilsudski died in 1935, I move out from Maciejowice to Warsaw till 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before that, you're now telling me that you moved from your town to Maciejowice. What-- why did you move from one town to another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was boring, then it's so much exciting in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Warsaw, when your family moved to Maciejowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, so I went to school to Maciejowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your whole family moved with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your father wanted to work in Maciejowice? It was better for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father-- my father had been travel all over in Poland. So once, every couple of months, he'd come to-- to home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was an easier life for your family to move to Maciejowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to elementary school--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maciejowice. Then when I move from Maciejowice to Warsaw, I went to special ed school, a night school, to learn German. Because that was-- in 1940 German occupied Poland. So that was mostly by-- by force, to go to German-- to school and learn German.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, still coming back to home, your father, you said was traveling a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was in charge of all the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your mother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kathryn, Kathryn Kolodziejek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is [? Dunek. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother come from the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe also she came from Wola Korycka. Maybe a different town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any aunts and uncles around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, my mom's sister, she lived in Grabnik. That's a big city, [NON-ENGLISH] live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she come to visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We come to visit her.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you sleep over in her house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did have children, I believe, two or three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Similar age to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you played together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now also, were you a religious family when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very religious, my mom, but me, I-- I just ignored them, escaped, as a kid, as a younger generation. Even I can tell you true, when at was Auschwitz, I forgot all about the God. Because we lived like a-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that as a child, did you go to church with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by force, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there name of the church in your town? Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The priest in his school, which was giving us lessons one-- one hour a week-- Lucian. Some way I remember. He was very good, strictly old-fashioned. He was very good to-- to help the poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- so he taught you good lessons as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but it was not so good, because usually I was-- bad behavior. So he drove me out in the hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't want to listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, or sometimes behind the blackboard. I mean, I'm telling true. Because, you know, I was young and easy going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like to study at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was good for-- lately for the history, for the religion, but not in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, as you got older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as-- as a child, you had problems in school with the teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other sisters and brothers, did they go to the same school with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they go in the lately to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They was much-- much younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went to school after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were never in school together with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- did you go on a steady basis when you went to church? Did your mom take the whole family and go to church on Sunday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, we go sometimes with the family, sometime with my brother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the church in your community do other functions, not on Sunday, but special functions for the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I believe so, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything that was very special to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 3rd of May, there was a big holiday, a procession. And a couple of days a year, there was special-- a special holiday, because St. Mary, special holiday in the evening. It was very, very, very exciting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to the procession? Did you take part?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I went. Because, you know, everything was so easy, so without control.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people of the town would come out to see the procession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you used to watch it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we joined too, or watch it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there beautiful costumes involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful costumes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the costumes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you got me. I don't know. I don't pay much attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said that your mother was a seamstress.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was excellent seamstress. Because she-- she sewing uniform for us, and she also is sewing for the neighborhood parents, even for-- my mom was very good to the Jewish family. Where she-- she'd buy-- buy the fabric from Jewish people, from Maciejowice. And I remember, I would-- we had three beautiful girls, Jewish, in the school. And they've been complaining on me. Because they was sitting on the front of me. They have beautiful hair. I pulled it-- I pulled the hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did your mother ever make any of the costumes for the processions since she was talented?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any holidays at home that were very special to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 3rd of May was special holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there special foods that were served?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special foods, oh, special holiday there was-- like here, on the Christmas time and Easter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was served in your house for Christmas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was food about for 13, 15, 16 different food on the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dishes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the important things that you remember that were very Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special bakery pastry, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] special, my mom bake it. She was excellent. And some kind of mushrooms, [INAUDIBLE] and pierogi with sauerkraut, or all kind of different, now delicious, delicious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she make in terms of meat? What were your specialties in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, she made an excellent-- my mom even killed the pork, and she made kielbasa, kishka, home-- home-- homemade, excellent. I never forget it. And goodies, goodies on the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sisters and brothers help? Did she do this all by herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children-- nothing, nothing, not even my brother, not even me. We only help to eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her life was not difficult? She had help.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she had help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your house. What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The truth, we used to live in a-- in a baron property, Petrosky. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had a beautiful life, like a castle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many rooms were in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own bedroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the color of the walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: White, blue, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a big bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toys, small horses, carriage; things like that. Also some balls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start collecting anything as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, I was not interested in nothing, nothing. Clearly I start-- when-- when I was liberated from Auschwitz. Then I woke up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we're still going to talk about your home life. Did you ever go on a trip with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Deblin. There was Air Force training for the army. And there was a big [NON-ENGLISH], when Marshal Pilsudski died in 1935 in May, we went to Deblin. Then after I went to see in Deblin, a beautiful city, so it was a dream for me to immigrate from-- from Maciejowice to Warsaw, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now in your town, what was the makeup of your town? You had Polish people living there, Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Polish-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea of statistics on how many Jews, how many Poles, lived in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maciejowice, it was a very well, the Jewish people. They owned beautiful stores, bakery, fish market, and it was a very, very nice town, Maciejowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: People lived well together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an area that Jews lived in, or did they live all over the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, mostly all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your house was in the town or outside of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the school was in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: School was in Maciejowice, in-- in-- in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walk. It was not far away, a mile and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had only one school in Maciejowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: And special seven class, there was one school, a big building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that, if somebody wanted to go on to another school, did they have an upper school?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have to transfer to Deblin, big city, or you even come to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took the road to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- who took you to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Even I don't know, but possible I went by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: With some friends-- my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there permanently with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to what kind of school did you go to in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a-- special for the very wealthy people, for the rich family who belonged to the Pilsudski or an organization, like a Legion Pilsudski. Although I was not from the poor-- from-- from the rich family-- I was like a poor normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were all boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: All boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was-- it was organized by a-- a wife of the-- [INAUDIBLE] president, she organize. And there was a couple hundred people.

speaker: interviewer
text: From all over Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all over Poland, mostly from a good family.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the children lived in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at private houses they lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a-- like a-- a-- West Point cadet.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like an academy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like academy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could sleep at the academy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a boarding?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd be working, cleaning the rooms, and making clean for the what you call, for the [NON-ENGLISH]. And keep-- keep in good shape, and tip-top everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of subjects did you study in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carpentry, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I was interested. But, everything was broke under the German occupy. So we were in different-- different-- different living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you study also history and sciences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just carpentry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody studied-- or did they have different interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different interests-- I was-- I was interested very much a little bit of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents come to visit you? Did you go back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went to parents, I visit home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often were you allowed to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe once a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through-- though special holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many years did you stay at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 19-- about five years, from 1935 to '40. But-- also Warsaw was occupied by German-- German army. So everything was broken, like everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came right in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all right, before we get to that, you said you were also starting-- starting to study German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's when the German come to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I work-- I work in the daytime for the [INAUDIBLE], the association with the barons from all over Europe, that's what they called it, [INAUDIBLE]. Incorporated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that's a German name, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Messenger, to-- to make them breakfast, and pick up their clothes, like a coat put on hanger, and delivering a message to the manual, or going to post office, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of the students work for this organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only me. I have this special job, by special recommendation for the barons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make good money doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had spending money in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spend mostly for the ice cream and chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: You liked ice cream. Did you go to the movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any movies you remember that were special that you liked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you reading at the time? Any special people that you loved to read? Any author that you enjoyed reading at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special things that you remember that you did on your time off that you enjoy doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traveling all around Poland-- not Poland-- Warsaw. Because before the war, Poland was beautiful. And so I traveled back and forth. And I had special pass for the buses and the-- and tramway, Saturdays and Sunday I travel for most often Wilanow. Wilanow, there's a castle. I get on at the castle in Wilanow, a beautiful museum. So I travel during the daytime, Saturday and Sunday. Because it costs me nothing, only time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- that was interest to see everything. So you knew the streets of Warsaw very well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, especially the old town, [INAUDIBLE]. Not far away from the-- from the palace, presidential palace. But a very historical and beautiful town, even now today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do remember the address of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [INAUDIBLE] Street. And they call it, like [INAUDIBLE], school of [INAUDIBLE], very important, for whatever the draft men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was near your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when you lived at your friend's house in Poland, do you happen to remember what street it was on?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I remember, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure, 16 or maybe about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make other friends while you were living--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was many friends. It was like a-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. That's for the-- for the-- for the poor people, for the poor children. That's also very disciplinary-- everybody must-- must obey. Everybody [BACKROUND NOISE] must come in on time. You had your own chapter. You would pay very little money, because it mostly was city was-- Warsaw pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Any special things that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's many, many people from Lvov, from Krakow, all the people which the-- for the university, for a different-- different training, I was between them. I was much younger than those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of going to the university in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but everything broke over the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the first moments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, excuse me. I was thinking I was going to join to the Polish army, let's say, there was meetings.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to join?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like West Point.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't have the chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me now the first sign that you realized something was happening in Warsaw in terms of the war. What was the very first thing that you remember that changed your life in Warsaw? [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] We're thinking, I think, around 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the German army come to Warsaw, I was scared to death [? and ?] those girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still living at your friend's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was living in the-- in the-- in that school. And [INAUDIBLE], the wife of the president.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the school when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many people was organized different way, some-- some-- a friend of mine been scared to-- about-- was thinking about joining the underground army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were also thinking about joining the underground army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the opportunity to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the opportunity, but I was too young, too naive. I was not even thinking about-- obviously is he going, I say nothing happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're-- you're at the school. The Germans are now occupying Warsaw. How long did you stay in the school after they occupied Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, maybe a couple of months. Then I-- completely-- it was like deserted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there from the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't show up anymore in the school. And I-- I still be working as messenger for the-- for the [INAUDIBLE]. And then I been living-- scary, scary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in a-- a place, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: On your own, by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, with that-- with that group. But many people been already arrested by German. And that was kind-- kind of scary place to live, even, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you-- did you hear anything from your family? Do you know what was happening to you my family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family used to live in Maciejowice, [? limited, ?] different town. They were safe there. But people who live Warsaw, it was very danger to live day by day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Germans start doing to the population in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- when the-- the city was bombed, very much, now this is. They-- the brought convoy, bread, giving bread to the people. And they're standing by the-- by the water, watching everybody take the water, for the cooking, for the drinks. It was organized not so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did-- were you on rations right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to wait on lines for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living now with boys from your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you all working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been working at-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had enough money to buy what you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I can manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could. Could the other boys manage too or was it hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been very-- I lost track, that many people had been arrested before me. And many people then I hear actings, who tried to kill German, [INAUDIBLE] too, because it was scary to live in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your friends started disappearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard they've been-- been killed in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they working already for the underground that they were killed in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a police, a martial law. They was not-- not allowed to walk in the evening. And they-- probably some friends of mine ignore it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not because they were doing something wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just walking on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just got shot. How many friends did this happen to that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many friends, OK. We'll stop the tape now.
